paradigms.name: Greedy
paradigms.coreIdea: At each step, make the locally optimal choice and never reconsider it. A greedy works when the problem has the greedy choice property -- a local optimum extends to a global optimum -- and optimal substructure.
whenItApplies: Problem asks for a minimum/maximum over a set of choices (scheduling, allocation, covering)
Each decision is irreversible and independent of future decisions
An exchange argument can prove that swapping any two adjacent choices can't improve the result
Interval problems, task scheduling, coin change with canonical denominations

subVariants.name: Sort-then-scan
subVariants.keyMechanism: Sort by one key, greedily consume in order
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Activity selection, meeting rooms

subVariants.name: Heap-maintained frontier
subVariants.keyMechanism: A priority queue tracks the best available choice at each step
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Dijkstra, Prim's MST, K closest points

subVariants.name: Exchange argument / inversion
subVariants.keyMechanism: Prove any deviation from greedy can be "uncrossed" without loss
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Job sequencing by deadline, Huffman coding

subVariants.name: Fractional relaxation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Take as much of the best item as fits, then next-best
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fractional knapsack

subVariants.name: Two-pass greedy
subVariants.keyMechanism: One left-to-right pass + one right-to-left pass, results combined
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Candy distribution, trapping rain water

subVariants.name: Lazy deletion greedy
subVariants.keyMechanism: Items are "removed" by marking rather than restructuring; heap pops skip stale entries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Task scheduler with cooldown, sliding window maximum

redFlags: The problem has a "0/1" or all-or-nothing choice (greedy usually fails -- DP needed)
Counter-examples exist where the locally best choice leads to a globally worse outcome
The problem involves dependencies between choices (use graph/DP instead)

paradigms.name: Dynamic Programming
paradigms.coreIdea: Break the problem into overlapping subproblems, solve each once, and store results. DP works when the problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems -- the hallmark distinction from divide-and-conquer.
whenItApplies: Counting, minimizing, maximizing over an exponential decision space
"How many ways", "minimum cost", "longest/shortest" phrasing
State can be compactly represented and transitions are well-defined
Choices interact (unlike greedy where they're independent)

subVariants.name: Top-down memoization
subVariants.keyMechanism: Recursive with a cache; natural for irregular state spaces
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word break, climbing stairs

subVariants.name: Bottom-up tabulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Iterative fill of a table in dependency order; avoids stack overhead
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Longest common subsequence, edit distance

subVariants.name: 1D rolling array
subVariants.keyMechanism: Current row depends only on previous row; reduce O(n^2) space to O(n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 0-1 knapsack (space-optimized)

subVariants.name: Interval DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State = (left, right) subarray or subrange; fill diagonally
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Matrix chain multiplication, burst balloons, palindrome partitioning

subVariants.name: Bitmask DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State includes a bitmask of which items have been used; handles small N with combinatorial choice
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Travelling salesman (small N), minimum XOR cover, assignment problems

subVariants.name: Digit DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State tracks position + tight constraint + accumulated property; counts integers in a range satisfying a property
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count numbers with digit sum = K in [L, R]

subVariants.name: Tree DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: DP runs on tree structure; state transitions along parent-child edges
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Max independent set on tree, diameter, rerooting technique

subVariants.name: Profile DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State encodes the "frontier" shape between processed and unprocessed; common in grid tiling
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Broken profile DP for domino tiling

subVariants.name: DP with monotone queue / divide & conquer optimization
subVariants.keyMechanism: Transition cost satisfies the concave/convex SMAWK condition; reduces O(n^2) to O(n log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Optimal BST, certain knapsack variants

redFlags: State space is too large to store (use heuristics or approximation instead)
No overlapping subproblems (pure divide-and-conquer is better)
Problem requires online decisions with no future lookahead (use greedy or sliding window)

paradigms.name: Divide and Conquer
paradigms.coreIdea: Split the problem into independent subproblems, solve recursively, then combine. Unlike DP, subproblems do not overlap -- solving them twice would give the same result.
whenItApplies: Problem naturally decomposes into halves or k-parts with no shared state
Combining partial solutions is efficient relative to solving the whole
The problem has a recursive geometric or structural self-similarity
"Find in sorted array", "build a balanced structure", "aggregate over a range"

subVariants.name: Binary search
subVariants.keyMechanism: Eliminate half the search space per step; requires monotone predicate
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Search in rotated array, find peak, capacity to ship packages

subVariants.name: Merge-based
subVariants.keyMechanism: Divide, recurse, merge with cross-partition contribution counted at merge
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Merge sort, count inversions, sort colors

subVariants.name: Randomized partition
subVariants.keyMechanism: Pick random pivot; expected O(n log n) or O(n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Quicksort, quickselect (kth largest)

subVariants.name: Recursive geometric
subVariants.keyMechanism: Partition space recursively; combine nearest-pair or range queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Closest pair of points, k-d tree construction

subVariants.name: Matrix exponentiation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Express recurrence as matrix multiply; fast-power gives O(log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fibonacci in O(log n), linear recurrence of fixed order

subVariants.name: Segment tree build
subVariants.keyMechanism: Build balanced tree over array; each node aggregates a range
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Range min/max/sum queries, lazy propagation

subVariants.name: Parallel binary search
subVariants.keyMechanism: Binary search simultaneously over multiple independent queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Offline range queries, persistent structures

redFlags: Subproblems are not independent (overlapping -- use DP)
Combining results is as expensive as solving the whole (no asymptotic gain)
Problem requires processing items in a specific order (use sweep line or DP)

paradigms.name: Graph Traversal
paradigms.coreIdea: Model the problem as a graph of states and edges, then traverse it to answer reachability, shortest path, ordering, or connectivity questions.
whenItApplies: Problem involves relationships, dependencies, or transitions between discrete states
"Can we reach X from Y?", "shortest sequence of moves", "ordering with dependencies"
The state space is naturally node-shaped (cells, nodes, configurations)
Implicit graphs where nodes are problem states and edges are valid transitions

subVariants.name: BFS (unweighted shortest path)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Level-by-level expansion; guarantees fewest edges
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word ladder, 0-1 BFS on grid, multi-source BFS

subVariants.name: Dijkstra (weighted shortest path)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Priority queue, always expand cheapest frontier node
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Network delay time, path with minimum effort

subVariants.name: Bellman-Ford
subVariants.keyMechanism: Relax all edges N-1 times; handles negative weights, detects negative cycles
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Cheapest flights within K stops, currency arbitrage

subVariants.name: DFS (topological / cycle detection)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Post-order DFS; reverse post-order = topological sort
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Course schedule, alien dictionary

subVariants.name: Tarjan's / Kosaraju's SCC
subVariants.keyMechanism: Two-pass DFS or stack-based; finds strongly connected components
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Critical connections, redundant connections

subVariants.name: Floyd-Warshall
subVariants.keyMechanism: All-pairs shortest paths; O(V^3) but simple
subVariants.archetypalProblem: All-pairs reachability on small graphs

subVariants.name: Bidirectional BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Expand from both source and target; meets in the middle
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word ladder (large graph), shortest path with huge branching factor

subVariants.name: 0-1 BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Deque instead of queue; handles edges of weight 0 or 1 only
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Minimum flips in grid, shortest path with free moves

subVariants.name: A*
subVariants.keyMechanism: BFS + admissible heuristic to guide expansion toward goal
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Pathfinding with spatial structure, 8-puzzle

subVariants.name: Implicit graph / state-space BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Nodes are (partial) problem states, not literal graph nodes
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Sliding puzzle, lock combination, BFS on game states

redFlags: Graph is too large to store explicitly (use implicit traversal or meet-in-the-middle)
Edge weights are real-valued and large (Dijkstra still works but consider precision)
Problem is about structure of the graph itself, not traversal (use Union-Find or matrix methods)

paradigms.name: Backtracking and Constraint Search
paradigms.coreIdea: Build a solution incrementally; abandon ("prune") partial solutions as soon as they violate constraints. Correct but potentially exponential -- pruning is everything.
whenItApplies: Problem asks to enumerate, find, or count all valid configurations
Constraints eliminate large portions of the search space early
No polynomial algorithm is known (NP-hard in general, tractable with pruning for small N)
"All permutations", "all subsets satisfying X", "place N queens", "solve this puzzle"

subVariants.name: Subset enumeration
subVariants.keyMechanism: Include/exclude each element; 2^N leaves
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Subsets, combination sum, power set

subVariants.name: Permutation generation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Fix element at each position; N! leaves
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Permutations, next permutation

subVariants.name: Constraint propagation (CSP-style)
subVariants.keyMechanism: After each assignment, propagate constraints to reduce domains
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Sudoku solver, N-queens with forward checking

subVariants.name: Branch and bound
subVariants.keyMechanism: Track best solution so far; prune branches that cannot beat it
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 0-1 knapsack (exact), TSP (exact)

subVariants.name: Iterative deepening DFS (IDDFS)
subVariants.keyMechanism: DFS with depth limit that increases; O(b^d) time, O(d) space
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Maze with unknown depth, 15-puzzle

subVariants.name: Meet in the middle
subVariants.keyMechanism: Split search space in half; solve each half, then join
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 4-sum, subset sum with large N, bidirectional backtrack

redFlags: N is large and pruning is weak (exponential blowup; use DP or approximation)
Problem only asks for one solution, not all (often DP or greedy is faster)
The constraint structure has a polynomial algorithm you haven't noticed yet

paradigms.name: Randomized Algorithms
paradigms.coreIdea: Use randomness to simplify the algorithm, avoid adversarial worst cases, or approximate hard problems. Correct in expectation or with high probability.
whenItApplies: Deterministic worst case is bad but expected case matters
Need to sample from a large or streaming population
Problem involves hashing, fingerprinting, or approximate membership
The adversary can break deterministic approaches (use randomized to deny worst-case inputs)

subVariants.name: Randomized quickselect
subVariants.keyMechanism: Random pivot; expected O(n) kth-element
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Kth largest element, median of unsorted array

subVariants.name: Reservoir sampling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Single pass over stream; each element has equal probability of being in sample
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Random sample from unknown-length stream

subVariants.name: Random hashing / universal hashing
subVariants.keyMechanism: Random hash function; low collision probability
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Distributed caching, frequency estimation

subVariants.name: Bloom filter
subVariants.keyMechanism: Hash into multiple bit positions; false positives possible, no false negatives
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Membership test with space constraint

subVariants.name: Count-Min Sketch
subVariants.keyMechanism: Array of hash-indexed counters; approximate frequency queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Heavy hitters in a stream

subVariants.name: Monte Carlo / Las Vegas
subVariants.keyMechanism: MC: fast, possibly wrong answer; LV: always correct, random runtime
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Primality testing (Miller-Rabin), randomized min-cut (Karger's)

subVariants.name: Skip list
subVariants.keyMechanism: Probabilistic layered linked list; O(log n) expected ops
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Ordered set with fast insert/delete, LRU cache variants

redFlags: Problem requires a guaranteed correct answer with a deterministic bound (use exact algorithm)
Randomness isn't available or seeding is adversarial
The problem size is small enough that deterministic O(n log n) is fine

paradigms.name: Mathematical and Number-Theoretic
paradigms.coreIdea: Exploit algebraic structure, number theory, or combinatorial identities to reduce an apparently hard problem to arithmetic -- often from O(n) to O(log n) or O(1).
whenItApplies: Problem involves modular arithmetic, divisibility, primes, or GCD structure
Counting problems with large N where direct enumeration is impossible
Geometric problems reducible to arithmetic (area, intersections, convex hull)
The answer involves factorials, Catalan numbers, Fibonacci, or similar sequences

subVariants.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes
subVariants.keyMechanism: Mark multiples; O(n log log n) prime generation
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count primes, prime factorization up to N

subVariants.name: GCD / LCM structure
subVariants.keyMechanism: Euclidean algorithm; Bezout's identity for linear combinations
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fraction simplification, water jug, minimum steps to make GCD

subVariants.name: Modular exponentiation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Fast power with modular reduction; O(log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Large Fibonacci, matrix power mod, RSA-style operations

subVariants.name: Combinatorics (nCr mod p)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Pascal's triangle or Lucas' theorem for modular binomial
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count valid paths in grid, distributing items

subVariants.name: Pigeonhole principle
subVariants.keyMechanism: If n items in k < n bins, some bin has >= 2
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Existence proofs, duplicate detection, calendar problems

subVariants.name: Prefix sum / difference array
subVariants.keyMechanism: Precompute cumulative sums; O(1) range query after O(n) build
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Subarray sum equals K, range update + query

subVariants.name: Convex hull trick
subVariants.keyMechanism: Envelope of linear functions; O(n) or O(n log n) DP optimization
subVariants.archetypalProblem: DP where cost is linear in a variable, CHT for slope optimization

subVariants.name: Geometric primitives
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cross product for orientation, Shoelace for area, sweep for intersections
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Convex hull, polygon area, point in polygon

redFlags: Problem doesn't have algebraic structure to exploit (use DP or graph instead)
Modular arithmetic introduces precision issues the problem doesn't accommodate
The number-theoretic reduction is more complex than the direct approach for the given constraints

paradigms.name: Simulation and State Machines
paradigms.coreIdea: Model the problem as a sequence of state transitions and execute them directly. No clever reduction -- the algorithm is the specification.
whenItApplies: Problem describes a process with explicit rules ("at each step, do X")
The state space is small enough to enumerate or the simulation terminates quickly
Finding cycles or steady states in a deterministic process
Event-driven systems where future events are triggered by current state

subVariants.name: Direct simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Execute the described process step by step; verify termination
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Game of Life, robot on a grid, simulate a stack/queue

subVariants.name: Cycle detection (Floyd / Brent)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Tortoise-and-hare or Brent's algorithm; find loop in O(n) time O(1) space
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Linked list cycle, find duplicate in array, hash function cycle

subVariants.name: Event-driven simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Priority queue of future events; advance time to next event
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Task scheduler, meeting room simulation, stock price events

subVariants.name: Finite automaton
subVariants.keyMechanism: Explicit state machine with transition table; process input character by character
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Regex matching (simple), string parsing, valid number

subVariants.name: Cellular automaton
subVariants.keyMechanism: Each cell updates based on neighbors; often run for K steps
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Conway's Game of Life, falling sand, spreading wildfire

subVariants.name: Physics / geometric simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Apply movement/collision rules repeatedly; check invariants
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Asteroid collision, dominoes falling, bouncing ball

redFlags: Simulation runs for too many steps (need closed-form math or cycle detection)
Problem asks for the optimal sequence of steps, not just the result of a given sequence (use DP or search)
State space is too large to track explicitly (use hashing or abstraction)

crossParadigmGuide.signal: "Does X exist?" with large search space
Answer is a single number derivable analytically
